**Monoamine oxidase B** (**MAO-B**) is an [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") that in humans is encoded by the *MAOB* [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene").

The protein encoded by this gene belongs to the flavin [monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") family. It is an [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") located in the [outer mitochondrial membrane](/wiki/Outer_mitochondrial_membrane "Outer mitochondrial membrane"). It catalyzes the [oxidative deamination](/wiki/Oxidative_deamination "Oxidative deamination") of biogenic and [xenobiotic](/wiki/Xenobiotic "Xenobiotic") [amines](/wiki/Amine "Amine") and plays an important role in the catabolism of neuroactive and vasoactive amines in the central nervous system and peripheral tissues. This protein preferentially degrades [benzylamine](/wiki/Benzylamine "Benzylamine") and [phenethylamine](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Similar to [monoamine oxidase A](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A "Monoamine oxidase A") (MAO-A), MAO-B is also involved in the catabolism of [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine").[[6]](#cite_note-Tan_2022-6)[*[contradictory](/wiki/Category:All_self-contradictory_articles "Category:All self-contradictory articles")*]

## Structure and function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_B&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure and function")]

MAO-B has a hydrophobic bipartite elongated cavity that (for the "open" conformation) occupies a combined volume close to 700 [Å](/wiki/%C3%85ngstr%C3%B6m "Ångström")3. hMAO-A has a single cavity that exhibits a rounder shape and is larger in volume than the "substrate cavity" of hMAO-B.[[7]](#cite_note-pmid17573034-7)

The first cavity of hMAO-B has been termed the *entrance cavity* (290 Å3), the second *substrate cavity* or *active site cavity* (~390 Å3) – between both an [isoleucine](/wiki/Isoleucine "Isoleucine")199 side-chain serves as a *gate*. Depending on the substrate or bound inhibitor, it can exist in either an open or a closed form, which has been shown to be important in defining the inhibitor specificity of hMAO-B. At the end of the substrate cavity is the [FAD](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") cofactor with sites for favorable amine binding about the flavin involving two nearly parallel tyrosyl (398 and 435) residues that form what has been termed an *aromatic cage*.[[7]](#cite_note-pmid17573034-7)

Like MAO-A, MAO-B catalyzes [O2](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen")-dependent oxidation of primary arylalkyl [amines](/wiki/Amines "Amines"), the initial step in the breakdown of these molecules. The products are the corresponding [aldehyde](/wiki/Aldehyde "Aldehyde"), [hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide"), and [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"):

:   Amine + O  
    2 + H  
    2O → Aldehyde + H  
    2O  
    2 + NH  
    3

This reaction is believed to occur in three steps. First, the amine is oxidized to the corresponding [imine](/wiki/Imine "Imine"), with reduction of the FAD cofactor to FADH2. Second, O2 accepts two electrons and two protons from FADH2, forming H  
2O  
2 and regenerating FAD. Third, the imine is [hydrolyzed](/wiki/Hydrolyzed "Hydrolyzed") by water, forming ammonia and the aldehyde.[[7]](#cite_note-pmid17573034-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Binda-8)

## Differences between MAO-A and MAO-B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_B&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Differences between MAO-A and MAO-B")]

MAO-A generally metabolizes [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine"), [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), and [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") (and other less clinically relevant chemicals). In contrast, MAO-B metabolizes dopamine[*[contradictory](/wiki/Category:All_self-contradictory_articles "Category:All self-contradictory articles")*] and [β-phenethylamine](/wiki/%CE%92-phenethylamine "Β-phenethylamine"), as well as other less clinically relevant chemicals.[[9]](#cite_note-Bortolato_2018-9) The differences between the substrate selectivity of the two enzymes are utilized clinically when treating specific disorders; MAO-A [inhibitors](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor "Monoamine oxidase inhibitor") have been typically used in the treatment of depression, whereas MAO-B inhibitors are typically used in the treatment of Parkinson's disease.[[10]](#cite_note-pmid8313400-10)[[11]](#cite_note-pmid22110357-11) Concurrent use of MAO-A inhibitors with [sympathomimetic](/wiki/Sympathomimetic_drug "Sympathomimetic drug") drugs can induce a [hypertensive crisis](/wiki/Hypertensive_crisis "Hypertensive crisis") as a result of excessive norepinephrine.[[12]](#cite_note-Calvi_2021-12) Likewise, the consumption of tyramine-containing substances, such as cheese, whilst using MAO-A inhibitors also carries the risk of hypertensive crisis.[[6]](#cite_note-Tan_2022-6)[[12]](#cite_note-Calvi_2021-12) Selective MAO-B inhibitors bypass this problem by preferentially inhibiting MAO-B, which allows tyramine to be metabolized freely by MAO-A in the [gastrointestinal tract](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_tract "Gastrointestinal tract").[[6]](#cite_note-Tan_2022-6)[[12]](#cite_note-Calvi_2021-12)

In 2021, it was discovered that MAO-A completely or almost completely mediates [striatal](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum") [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism") in the rodent brain and that MAO-B is not importantly involved.[[13]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-14) In contrast, MAO-B appears to mediate [γ-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/%CE%93-aminobutyric_acid "Γ-aminobutyric acid") (GABA) synthesis from [putrescine](/wiki/Putrescine "Putrescine") in the striatum, a minor and alternative [metabolic pathway](/wiki/Metabolic_pathway "Metabolic pathway") of GABA synthesis, and this synthesized GABA in turn inhibits [dopaminergic](/wiki/Dopaminergic "Dopaminergic") [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") in this brain area.[[13]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-14)[[15]](#cite_note-WatanabeMaemuraKanbara2002-15) MAO-B specifically mediates the [transformations](/wiki/Biotransformation "Biotransformation") of putrescine into [γ-aminobutyraldehyde](/wiki/%CE%93-aminobutyraldehyde "Γ-aminobutyraldehyde") (GABAL or GABA aldehyde) and [*N*-acetylputrescine](/wiki/N-acetylputrescine "N-acetylputrescine") into [*N*-acetyl-γ-aminobutyraldehyde](/wiki/N-acetyl-%CE%B3-aminobutyraldehyde "N-acetyl-γ-aminobutyraldehyde") (*N*-acetyl-GABAL or *N*-acetyl-GABA aldehyde).[[15]](#cite_note-WatanabeMaemuraKanbara2002-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Seiler2004-16)[[13]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-14) These findings may warrant a rethinking of the actions of [MAO-B inhibitors](/wiki/MAO-B_inhibitor "MAO-B inhibitor") in the treatment of [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[13]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-14)

## Roles in disease and aging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_B&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roles in disease and aging")]

[Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease") (AD) and [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease") (PD) are both associated with elevated levels of MAO-B in the brain.[[17]](#cite_note-pmid7816197-17)[[18]](#cite_note-pmid19526285-18) The normal activity of MAO-B creates [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species"), which directly damage cells.[[19]](#cite_note-pmid17447416-19) MAO-B levels have been found to increase with age, suggesting a role in [natural age related cognitive decline](/wiki/Memory_and_aging "Memory and aging") and the increased likelihood of developing neurological diseases later in life.[[20]](#cite_note-pmid15247489-20) More active [polymorphisms](/wiki/Polymorphism_(biology) "Polymorphism (biology)") of the MAO-B gene have been linked to negative [emotionality](/wiki/Emotionality "Emotionality"), and suspected as an underlying factor in [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)").[[21]](#cite_note-pmid19657584-21) Activity of MAO-B has also been shown to play a role in stress-induced cardiac damage.[[22]](#cite_note-pmid20869994-22)[[23]](#cite_note-pmid23581564-23) Over-[expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") and increased levels of MAO-B in the brain have also been linked to the accumulation of amyloid β-peptides ([Aβ](/wiki/Amyloid_beta "Amyloid beta")), through mechanisms of the [amyloid precursor protein secretase](/wiki/Amyloid_precursor_protein_secretase "Amyloid precursor protein secretase"), [γ-secretase](/wiki/Gamma_secretase "Gamma secretase"), responsible for the development of plaques, observed in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's patients. Evidence suggests that [siRNA](/wiki/Small_interfering_RNA "Small interfering RNA") [silencing](/wiki/Gene_silencing "Gene silencing") of MAO-B, or [inhibition](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor "Enzyme inhibitor") of MAO-B through MAO-B inhibitors ([Selegline](/wiki/Selegiline "Selegiline"), [Rasagiline](/wiki/Rasagiline "Rasagiline")), slows the progression, improves and reverses the symptoms, associated with AD and PD, including the reduction of [Aβ](/wiki/Amyloid_beta "Amyloid beta") plaques in the brain.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Animal models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_B&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Animal models")]

Transgenic mice that are unable to produce MAO-B are shown to be resistant to a mouse model of Parkinson's disease.[[26]](#cite_note-pmid10591056-26)[[27]](#cite_note-pmid9326944-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) They also demonstrate increased responsiveness to stress (as with MAO-A [knockout mice](/wiki/Knockout_mouse "Knockout mouse"))[[29]](#cite_note-Shih_21–30-29) and increased [β-PEA](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine").[[27]](#cite_note-pmid9326944-27)[[29]](#cite_note-Shih_21–30-29) In addition, they exhibit behavioral disinhibition and reduced anxiety-like behaviors.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Treatment with [selegiline](/wiki/Selegiline "Selegiline"), an MAO-B inhibitor, in rats has been shown to prevent many age-related biological changes, such as [optic nerve](/wiki/Optic_nerve "Optic nerve") [degeneration](/wiki/Neurodegeneration "Neurodegeneration"), and extend average lifespan by up to 39%.[[31]](#cite_note-pmid23082958-31)[[32]](#cite_note-pmid8423709-32) However, subsequent research suggests that the anti-aging effects of selegiline in animals are due to its [catecholaminergic activity enhancer](/wiki/Catecholaminergic_activity_enhancer "Catecholaminergic activity enhancer") actions rather than MAO-B inhibition.[[33]](#cite_note-Miklya2016-33)

### Effects of deficiency in humans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_B&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Effects of deficiency in humans")]

While people lacking the gene for MAO-A display [intellectual disabilities](/wiki/Intellectual_disabilities "Intellectual disabilities") and behavioral abnormalities, people lacking the gene for MAO-B display no abnormalities except elevated phenethylamine levels in urine.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[9]](#cite_note-Bortolato_2018-9)

The prophylactic use of MAO-B inhibitors to slow natural human aging in otherwise healthy individuals has been proposed, but remains a highly controversial topic.[[35]](#cite_note-pmid20150659-35)[[36]](#cite_note-pmid15246996-36)

Selegiline is a selective MAO-B at low doses and a non-selective MAOI at higher doses; it also induces dopaminergic activity at low doses. This different action, depending on the dose, implies different use: low doses (up to 10 mg/day) for Parkinson's disease and higher doses as antidepressant treatment (Table 1) (55). ... Higher doses of oral and transdermal selegiline have been linked to a major frequency of orthostatic hypotension (227). No hypertensive crisis was reported with patch administration, but a small portion of patients with preexisting hypertension showed a worse BP control (224)."
